1: Moses and the elders give the charge together — the plurality of authorities delivering the commandment. The same communal delivery pattern appears in Acts 15:22-23 (the Jerusalem Council's letter sent by apostles and elders together), and ultimately in the commissioning of the church: Jesus's authority is expressed through the apostolic community he sends (Matthew 28:18-20: 'all authority has been given to me ... therefore go'). The continuity of Moses's authority into the Promised Land through the covenant document anticipates Christ's words enduring past his physical presence: 'my words will never pass away' (Matthew 24:35).
2: The large stones set up on the day of Jordan-crossing are a monument of covenant entry. Joshua 4's twelve memorial stones from the Jordan itself enact the same logic. Baptism in the NT is the Jordan-crossing moment — the passage from the old life to the new, marked by a public declarative act. Peter explicitly connects the flood/water-deliverance pattern to baptism (1 Peter 3:21: 'this water symbolizes baptism that now saves you'); the Jordan crossing is the same type pattern applied to Israel's covenant entry.
3: Writing the law on plastered stones was the ancient ANE treaty-inscription practice — Hittite suzerainty treaties were written on stone and deposited at the sanctuary. This law inscribed on stone is what Paul contrasts with the new-covenant inscription: '2 Corinthians 3:3: written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts.' The stone inscription is visible and external; the Spirit's inscription is internal and transformative. Jeremiah 31:33 announces the shift: 'I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts.'
4: The stones are set up specifically on Mount Ebal — the mountain of curse (Deuteronomy 27:13), not Gerizim (the mountain of blessing). The law inscribed at the place of curse is a visual theology: the covenant document stands permanently at the location of covenant failure's consequence. Paul reads this in Galatians 3:10-13: the law pronounces a curse on all who fail to keep it, and all have failed. Christ 'redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' — which Paul anchors in Deuteronomy 21:23. The Ebal stones bear the law; Christ bore the curse the law pronounces.
5: 'There you shall build an altar to the LORD your God out of stones that no iron tool has touched' — the uncut stone altar preserves the altar's natural, creation character: no human craftsmanship mediates between the worshipper and YHWH. This ban on iron-tooled stones echoes Exodus 20:25 and is the opposite of the idol-carving indicted throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 4:28: 'the work of human hands'). Christ as 'the stone the builders rejected' (Psalms 118:22; Matthew 21:42; Acts 4:11) is the divinely-appointed cornerstone that no human craftsman fashioned: 'the stone was cut out, but not by human hands' (Daniel 2:45).
6: The burnt offerings on the Ebal altar are total-consecration offerings — entirely given to God, nothing retained by the worshipper. Christ's offering of himself is the ultimate burnt offering: 'he offered himself unblemished to God' (Hebrews 9:14), entirely given, nothing held back. The altar at the curse-mountain receiving burnt offerings anticipates the cross at Golgotha — the place of curse and shame that becomes the site of the most complete self-offering in history. The worshippers who bring their offerings will eat peace offerings here (Deuteronomy 27:7) — but the burnt offering itself is wholly consumed.
7: Peace offerings (šĕlāmîm) are shared meals: part to YHWH (the fat and blood), part to the priest, part to the offerer and family. The joy of eating in YHWH's presence on Mount Ebal — the curse mountain — is a remarkable image: the curse-location becomes the feast-location. The Lord's Supper participates in this dynamic: Jesus institutes the meal on the night of his betrayal, in the shadow of the cross — the feast is shared at the curse-location, and the eating is an act of covenant joy in the presence of the one who bore the curse (1 Corinthians 10:16: 'is not the cup a participation in the blood of Christ?').
8: 'Write all the words of this law on the stones very clearly' (bāʾēr hêṭēb, inscribe with utmost clarity) — the law's clarity is not for decoration but for accountability: no one could claim ignorance. Paul's reflection on the law's clarity runs throughout Romans 1-3: the law makes the knowledge of sin precise ('through the law we become conscious of our sin,' Romans 3:20; 'I would not have known what sin was had it not been for the law,' Romans 7:7). The clarity of the stone inscription is the clarity that condemns; the Gospel's clarity (2 Corinthians 3:12-13) is the clarity that transforms.
9: 'Be silent, Israel, and listen — today you have become the people of the LORD your God' — the Levitical proclamation of covenant constitution at the moment of Jordan-crossing is the prototype declaration. Jesus's baptism (Matthew 3:16-17) mirrors the structure: the Spirit descends, and the divine voice declares 'This is my Son, my beloved' — the covenant-constitution of the new covenant community in its Head. The silence commanded before the announcement reflects the gravity of the covenantal declaration; similarly, Jesus's transfiguration elicits the command 'Listen to him!' (Matthew 17:5), the same posture of attentive silence before the divine address.
10: 'Obey the LORD your God by keeping his commandments and statutes' — the covenant obligation that flows from the new identity declared in Deuteronomy 27:9. The sequence is grace-then-law (identity-then-obligation), not law-then-grace, which is why Paul argues in Galatians 3:17-18 that the promise to Abraham was not annulled by the law given 430 years later. Jesus's 'if you love me, keep my commands' (John 14:15) follows the same sequence: the love-relationship precedes and grounds the obedience, not vice versa.
11: The division of tribes for blessing and curse is programmatic: Moses sets up the ceremony before it occurs, and Joshua 8:30-35 records its fulfillment. The word-event structure — the command given before entry, fulfilled upon entry — reflects the prophetic pattern by which YHWH's word shapes history before it unfolds. Jesus's pre-announcement of his death and resurrection (Matthew 16:21; Mark 8:31; Luke 9:22) is the same structure: the word precedes and interprets the event, so that when it occurs the disciples may believe (John 14:29: 'I have told you before it happens, so that when it does happen you will believe').
12: The six tribes on Gerizim for blessing — Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin — are all sons of the two full wives (Leah and Rachel), with the exception of Simeon. Judah is among the blessing tribes; the Messiah comes from Judah (Genesis 49:10: 'the scepter will not depart from Judah'). The geographical staging of blessing from Mount Gerizim anticipates the beatitudes: Jesus pronounces covenant blessings on those who embody the covenant character (Matthew 5:3-12), himself standing as the mediator of blessing rather than the one who merely recites it.
13: The six curse tribes on Ebal include Reuben (who forfeited the firstborn right, Genesis 49:4), Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali — the tribes of the concubines plus Reuben, Zebulun, and Gad. The curse-side staging does not permanently define these tribes' destiny; in Revelation 7:4-8 all twelve tribes are sealed, including those that stood at Ebal. Christ's work of redemption covers both sides: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13) — there is no tribe beyond the reach of the atonement.
14: The Levites call out the curses 'in a loud voice' (qôl rām) — the covenant sanctions are proclaimed publicly, unmistakably, to the entire gathered people. This public proclamation of the curse is the precedent for the NT's frank acknowledgment that all are under condemnation: 'there is no one righteous, not even one' (Romans 3:10-18). The loudness of the Levitical curse-proclamation is the equivalent of Paul's 'all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God' (Romans 3:23) — the diagnosis that creates the need for the cure. The Gospel's good news is unintelligible apart from this loud announcement of the bad news.
15: 'Cursed is the man who carves or casts an idol and sets it up in secret' — idol worship in secret is the act of divided loyalty: external covenant membership combined with private spiritual allegiance elsewhere. Jesus applies the idolatry-in-secret principle to the spiritual realm: 'no one can serve two masters' (Matthew 6:24); 'your eye is the lamp of your body ... if your eyes are unhealthy, your whole body will be full of darkness' (Matthew 6:22-23). The hiddenness of secret idolatry makes it the most corrupting form: it evades social accountability while corrupting the worshipper from within. Revelation condemns the 'Nicolaitans' and 'Jezebel' teaching for this same hidden compromise (Revelation 2:14-15, 20-21).
16: 'Cursed is anyone who treats his father or mother with contempt' — the honor-parents command (the fifth commandment, Deuteronomy 5:16) is here expressed in its negative covenant-curse form. Jesus sharpens this to include the internal attitude, not just external act: anger and contempt are equivalent to murder in the Sermon on the Mount's logic (Matthew 5:21-22). More pointedly, Jesus condemns the Corban practice (Mark 7:9-13) in which scribal tradition allowed property-dedication to YHWH as an excuse to avoid supporting parents — a technical workaround that violated the spirit of the honor-commandment while satisfying its letter.
17: 'Cursed is anyone who moves his neighbor's boundary marker' — the property boundary as the sacred delimiter of each family's covenant portion in the land. Moving a boundary stone was a silent fraud that compounded over generations. The prophets condemn this specifically (Isaiah 5:8: 'woe to you who add house to house and field to field until there is room for no one else'; Micah 2:2). Jesus's parable of the prodigal son in reverse — the elder son who sees his father's estate as permanently fixed — and the warnings against accumulation (Luke 12:15-21) address the same acquisitive impulse that boundary-moving served. Christ embodies the opposite: he 'became poor so that you through his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9).
18: 'Cursed is anyone who leads a blind person astray on the road' — the exploitation of physical disability is the particular test of community character. Leviticus 19:14 prohibits this alongside the prohibition of cursing the deaf. Jesus responds to the opposite imperative: he heals the blind rather than exploiting them (Matthew 9:27-30; John 9). More broadly, he characterizes his mission as restoring sight to the blind (Luke 4:18, quoting Isaiah 61:1) — the Isaianic promise of the messianic age when the curse of disability exploitation will be reversed.
19: 'Cursed is anyone who denies justice to the foreigner, the fatherless, or the widow' — the covenant justice triad that appears throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 10:18; Deuteronomy 14:29; Deuteronomy 24:17-21; Deuteronomy 26:12-13). The curse formulation makes the denial of justice to the vulnerable not merely unethical but covenantally destructive. James 1:27 defines true religion as 'looking after orphans and widows in their distress,' applying the Deuteronomic social obligation to the new covenant community. Jesus's judgment parable (Matthew 25:31-46) bases the verdict on treatment of 'the least of these' — the hungry, thirsty, stranger, and naked — the NT equivalents of the foreigner-fatherless-widow triad.
20: 'Cursed is anyone who sleeps with his father's wife' — the violation of the father's sexual boundary, which is both an incest law and a property-and-honor violation (the father's wife was under his household authority). Paul directly addresses this in 1 Corinthians 5:1-2 when a Corinthian believer 'has his father's wife' — Paul demands covenant expulsion, applying the covenantal gravity of Deuteronomy 27:20 to the new covenant community. The specific phrasing 'violated his father's marriage' (gicceh kĕnap ʾābîw, uncovered his father's garment) is the cloak-coverage violation of Deuteronomy 23:1 — the same garment-image appears in Ruth 3:9 and Ezekiel 16:8 for the covenant-covering of marriage.
21: 'Cursed is anyone who has sexual relations with any animal' — the boundary between human and animal is covenantally ordered: image-bearers are not to violate the Creator's design-distinctions. While this specific prohibition requires little christological elaboration, the general principle of design-boundary respect is grounded in creation theology: 'in the beginning' God made them male and female (Genesis 1:27; Deuteronomy 2:24) — the Creator's design that Jesus himself cites as the authoritative grounding for marriage ethics (Matthew 19:4-6). Sexual ethics in both testaments are anchored in the Creator's original design, not merely social convention.
22: 'Cursed is anyone who sleeps with his sister, whether she is his father's daughter or his mother's daughter' — half-siblings (same father, different mother) are included; this closes the loophole by which Abraham married his half-sister Sarah (Genesis 20:12) under a different era's norms. The comprehensiveness of the incest prohibition reflects the covenant's ordering of the family as the foundational covenant unit. Paul's catalog of sexual immorality in 1 Corinthians 5-7 and his general principle 'flee sexual immorality' (1 Corinthians 6:18) are the NT expression of the same covenant-family-protection logic.
23: 'Cursed is anyone who sleeps with his mother-in-law' — the extension of incest prohibitions to the in-law relationships recognizes that marriage creates new family bonds that carry the same kinship obligations. Jesus's teaching on the indissolubility of marriage (Matthew 19:6: 'what God has joined together, let no one separate') is the positive counterpart to the curse-prohibitions: the covenant bond creates genuine kinship that the community must protect, not merely the biological kinship. The marriage union is so profound that violations of its boundaries at the in-law level are covenant violations, not merely social impropriety.
24: 'Cursed is anyone who secretly attacks his neighbor' — the Levite's loud public curse specifically targets secret violence: the attack that evades communal witness and accountability. Jesus's warning about the 'darkness' of hidden deeds (John 3:20: 'everyone who does evil hates the light and will not come into the light for fear that their deeds will be exposed') addresses the same psychology of secret sin. The resurrection's judgment is the ultimate exposure of all hidden things: 'nothing is hidden that will not be disclosed, and nothing concealed that will not be known or brought out into the open' (Luke 8:17).
25: 'Cursed is anyone who accepts a bribe to kill an innocent person' — judicial corruption that weaponizes the court system against the innocent. The passion narrative fulfills this curse-scenario literally: Judas accepts thirty pieces of silver to deliver Jesus to the authorities (Matthew 26:15; cf. Zechariah 11:12-13), and the Sanhedrin uses the legal apparatus to secure a death sentence against one they know to be innocent (Matthew 26:59-60: 'they were looking for false evidence against Jesus ... but they did not find any'). The curse of bribed judicial murder falls on those who engineered the crucifixion; ironically, Christ's death under that unjust sentence is the means of atoning for the very sin the curse addresses.
26: 'Cursed is anyone who does not uphold the words of this law by carrying them out' — the closing, all-encompassing curse that makes the entire law a binary: either perfect compliance or the curse. Paul quotes this verse in Galatians 3:10 as the proof that all who rely on the law are under a curse: 'for it is written, Cursed is everyone who does not continue to do everything written in the Book of the Law.' Paul's argument proceeds: since no one can keep the law perfectly, all stand under this curse — therefore 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). This final Ebal curse is the most theologically decisive verse in the chapter: it is Paul's diagnosis that makes the Gospel's cure necessary.
